text: Good morning, Vip. It's Geoff Coghlan here from the Wesley Hospital in Brisbane, Australia, and I've got John Yaxley here with me. Hi, Vip. Congratulations on your ten-thousandth case. It's a wonderful effort. Congratulations. Yeah, Vip, it's great to be part of your day today. We're very proud of your achievements and all you've done for our specialty and robotics internationally. Congratulations. And we also wanted you to invite us to present this case of Geoff's. Thank you.

is a young man, he's fifty-two, PSA at five point seven, he's got palpable disease at the left apex and mid regions of his prostate and has high volumes of Gleason four plus three adenocarcinoma in that area and the rest of his prostate's clear on biopsy. And we have a PSMA PET scan here that shows uptake in that region with no evidence of disease elsewhere.
And we'll just start our broadcast from now after the bladder's been taken down.

The surgeon begins by making a lateral incision at the base of the prostate to cut through the endopelvic fascia, aiming to expose the raw red muscle fibers of the levator ani by passing through the two layers of fascia.

The surgeon maintains a plane that provides a clear view and mobility of the prostate, while leaving the pubic prostate ligaments intact initially. The ligaments will be divided later along with the dorsal venous complex.

The surgeon proceeds through the bladder neck, employing a pinch maneuver with both hands to assess the contour of the prostate gland's base. The opened endopelvic fascia is visible, and the surgeon begins the incision at the bladder neck.

The surgeon uses diathermy to control vessels at the anterior part of the bladder neck, leaving detrusor fibers intact. Bipolar or monopolar cautery is applied to manage bleeding, ensuring to stay in the midline to avoid larger veins.

So assistant-wise, you've got a nurse practitioner assistant. Do you have any comments on nurse practitioner's assisting? Look, when I was with VIP and he had a physician's assistant that he'd done a lot of cases with, I realised early on that I succeeded well with her assisting me and not as well if I had a new fellow working with me. And the importance of a skilled assistant was obvious. So the easiest way to get an ongoing skilled assistant nursing staff and Sheree who's assisting me today we've done probably fifteen hundred cases together and I'm certainly most comfortable operating with her.

The surgeon has completed the incision through the anterior bladder neck and proceeds with apical dissection, approaching from the side with no disease. The aim is to perform a high release of the right neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon visualizes the apical anatomy and performs sharp dissection to divide the dorsal vein. The urethra is divided, and the apex is lifted. The surgeon plans an incremental left-sided nerve-sparing approach due to concerns of T-III disease. The prostatic fascia is opened, and the surgeon aims to leave a fascial layer on the prostate, staying interfascial to avoid exposing raw, white prostate tissue.

The surgeon aims to perform a high release at the apex of the prostate, obtaining a clear view of the anterior apex to prepare for dividing the urethra and dorsal vein. Venous bleeding occurs, and the assistant uses a suction device to apply pressure and manage the bleeding while the surgeon continues to identify the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon performs a partial nerve sparing procedure on the side with capsular abutment, carefully dissecting through the dorsal venous complex. The decision between incremental and total resection of the neurovascular bundle is based on disease grade and imaging findings.

The surgeon performs a nerve-sparing technique on the right side, working from right to left around the apex of the prostate. The dorsal vein is sharply dissected, with the surgeon focusing on identifying the shoulder of the apex of the prostate while maintaining anatomical awareness.

And what suture material are you going to use now for the DVC ligation? I normally use a six inch V-lock. I would like to have some single arm quill sutures, but we don't have them at present. It's really one of the only places in the operation where I use V-lock. I find it's a bit rough to use on the urethra, but it's quite simple to use for the dorsal vein. Well, we've got a bit of time. This patient has not... You've decided not to do a lymph node dissection on this patient. Any thoughts on which lymph node... which patients you perform pelvic lymph node dissections on and whether you perform an extended or a standard node dissection?

Yeah, node dissections, an ongoing debate, really, I think, and I have no doubt that it increases the morbidity of the operation. I only do extended node dissections. Mm-hm. I think its main benefit is as a staging procedure. So I will do it in young patients with high-risk disease or if it's someone like this with Gleason four plus three, I'll typically only do it if their PSA is much higher. I think my chances of getting positive nodes on him are low and I'm less likely to do it on people that are older where I'm very unlikely to use salvage or adjuvant radiotherapy. Once patients get to seventy, I'm generally, if I do a radical prostatectomy, that would be the only local treatment I give them. So the staging benefits of knowing nodal status in that situation, I think, are less. And how has the PET-PSMA scan influenced your decision on node dissection? We do use a lot of PET-PSMA staging here in Brisbane. What are your thoughts on that? Look, PET-PSMA is certainly an advance in staging of disease. I think what we're seeing internationally and certainly at our centre with a big experience is it's a status but its sensitivity is poor so certainly if we see positive nodes on the PSMA we will usually see much more significant nodal involvement histologically than we see radiologically but negative nodal staging on PSMA is not particularly reassuring in high-risk disease where we still have a reasonable percentage of patients returning positive nodes with a node dissection.

The surgeon begins ligation of the dorsal venous complex (DVC), working from lateral to medial and posterior to anterior. The technique involves starting at the right-hand side, taking superficial suture bites across the DVC, and using diathermy to control significant arterial bleeding.

And what pressure do you work at in the pneumoperitoneum? In patients like this that are young and fit, I work with it on fifteen. If they're older patients or I'm a bit worried about them in some way, I'll drop it to ten and I don't find a big difference in the bleeding at that point. So if you can explain what you're doing here, Jeff, with this anterior bladder. Yeah, so I'll put a suture through this uracus. I did this at the very beginning of my consultant experience after my fellowship with VIP, really just to try and reduce the burden on my assistant when my assistant was inexperienced. useful, it's simple and quick to do and we also use a long lateral assistant port and if you pull this suture back through the right upper quadrant assistant port you get a nice bridge over the peritoneal contents with a long assistant port and I feel it's a bit safer when your assistants are traversing the abdomen out of vision. Talking about instruments, we've got two robots at our institution, an SI and an XI. Any preference for prostates between those technologies? I love the XI. The SI is my least favourite of the three robots that I use. I prefer to be on the S than this. I don't use the SI very much, only a few times a year. Today is unfortunately one of those days, but I'd much rather be on the XI.

The surgeon encounters an adhesion during the prostatectomy procedure, which has caused difficulties earlier in the operation. The surgeon considers addressing the adhesion now, noting the dexterity advantages of using the XI robotic system for such maneuvers.

The surgeon begins apical dissection from the nerve-sparing side, having already divided the dorsal vein for increased prostate mobility. The Denonvilliers' fascia is visible at the back, and the surgeon works to separate the rectum from the apex of the prostate.

The surgeon navigates the plane around the prostate, ensuring the neurovascular bundle is adequately retracted to expose the apex of the prostate at its junction with the urethra. A fascia layer remains over the prostate, and the surgeon carefully avoids over-dissection of the urethra, maintaining a margin of a few millimeters from the apex.

The surgeon performs a bilateral nerve sparing procedure, freeing the left side before cutting through the urethra. The capsular artery is divided, and small Asculap metallic clips are applied to control bleeding near the urethra.

The surgeon performs dissection of the posterior bladder neck, noting the increased mobility of the prostate. The anterior bladder neck is divided at the start of the procedure to avoid getting too close during retrograde dissection.

The surgeon performs posterior blandic dissection, carefully avoiding lateral areas where vessels are located. A broad plane is established, with a small adenoma visible in the middle. The mucosal lining of the bladder is observed, indicating the angle and thickness of the bladder. The surgeon leaves detrusor fibers on the prostate while dissecting until vertical fibers are visible, signaling the transition to more anterior dissection. Good retraction with the left hand aids in separating the tissues without excessive force.

The surgeon begins dissecting through the vertical fibers, revealing a small amount of fat laterally. The dissection is carefully positioned a few millimeters away from the prostate to avoid entering the plane between the prostate, vesicles, and vas deferens. The anterior surface of the vas and vesicles is targeted for dissection.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to carefully separate the neurovascular bundle from the base of the prostate, ensuring the structures are moved laterally. Metal clips are applied to secure the area, avoiding excessive stripping of the capsule.

The surgeon employs an athermal technique to minimize thermal energy use while dissecting through the bladder neck. The prostate is visualized, and the surgeon avoids getting too close to the prostatic fascia before applying clips. Minimal bleeding is observed, and any bleeding that occurs is tolerated unless it obscures the surgical view.

The surgeon controls the pedicles and tolerates back bleeding while working slightly off the prostate. More tissue is taken down compared to the VIP technique, with clips applied on either side in a smaller space to minimize back bleeding.

The surgeon dissects the seminal vesicle, starting with the vas deferens and working medially. The dissection is performed on the medial edge of the seminal vesicle, with the rectum covered by fascia. The surgeon attempts to connect this dissection to the initial anterior dissection through a tunnel in the middle, while maintaining a view that avoids potential rectal injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects lateral to the seminal vesicle and medial to the prostatic pedicle, aiming to join this plane with the posterior dissection. The neurovascular bundle is being separated from the prostate, and Asculap clips are applied to visible vessels to control bleeding, preparing to release the tissue down to a small pedicle for hemolob clip placement.

The surgeon incises sharply into the neurovascular bundle on the opposite side, aiming to leave some tissue on the prostate while carefully dissecting around the base. The vesicles are left intact until the final step of dissection, unless disease is present at the base, which would require lifting the vesicles.

The surgeon navigates around the lateral border of the prostate, aiming to leave half of the neurovascular tissue on the patient and half on the prostate. The setup on the left side is more challenging, especially when the apex is released, requiring careful identification of the lateral plane.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to elevate the prostate, enhancing its mobility. The inferior part of the neurovascular bundle is directly divided, avoiding placement of clips too close to the urethra. Small titanium clips are applied distally once the prostate is removed, while Hemolox clips are used at the base of the prostate.

The surgeon prepares to divide the seminal vesicles and vas deferens, using clips to secure these structures. The bilateral nerve-sparing technique is employed, aiming to reduce traction during the operation by releasing the pedicle and neurovascular bundle before this step.

The surgeon lifts the tissue on one side, applying a couple of clips to complete the dissection on that side. The neurovascular bundle is visible, with some tissue remaining down the left side, maintaining an interfacial approach without intrafacial involvement on the prostate.

Now the plan to reconstruct, any steps to take through from here? No, I really do what VIP's shown us all and I'll just make sure I'm happy with vascular control first and then I'll do a posterior reconstruction and an anastomosis, really like we see VIP. The only difference I have is with my anastomosis, I now generally just do anterior racket handle reconstructions where I think VIP still does lateral fish mouth I should say I know sometimes most times you can get the bladder down quite easily but there's the occasion where it is quite tense getting the bladder to the urethra do you revert to a different type of bladder technique that stage or you've just found that you've always been able to get it down? Yeah I really haven't had a lot of trouble ever getting the bladder down I probably sure I've got reasonable mobilization laterally might reduce my pneumoperitoneum a little bit or my Trendelenburg a little bit make sure I do a posterior suture reconstruction and if that's not bringing the bladder down then I would potentially do a posterior racket handle so my masmosis is a little closer but I've never actually had to do that

The surgeon addresses bleeding in the bilateral nerve-sparing region, opting to control small distal vessel bleeding near the apex using flow seal and compression rather than suturing. The left side shows some bleeding, which is managed with minimal fascial manipulation.

If I'm on the XI, then I stay that way. If I need the view, I'll just flip it to thirty up. With the SI, I'll normally go back to a straight lens to do the anastomosis. You find some patients you see well with a thirty down and I just make the decision on individual merits. But in general principle, you'd still use the zero degree for the, for the apex anastomosis? Generally, yeah. If you had a vessel like that at the apex near a vesca bundle, is that something you would leave if it was at the apex? Normally I'd leave that and at the end I'd put some flow seal and a moist Raytec in and just leave it under a little bit of compression for five minutes and see if that controls it. I think you can always salvage that situation. These vessels you can't salvage post anastoma. you can wash that out and then put a little suture down there at the very end. We seem to have a shortage of sutures today. So I'm just waiting for a stitch. And you use, what stitch will you use for this, Geoff? Oh, three-o monocryl on an RB-I has been my go-to suture. very familiar with the needle. I think the monofilament's nice with the newer machines compared to braided sutures.

The surgeon observes a small area on the diaphragm where previous attempts to control bleeding were made. The surgical field appears dry, but the surgeon considers using flow seal and moist Raytec for compression if any bleeding is detected.

The surgeon holds up the free edge of Denonvilliers' fascia after cutting through with the seminal vesicles (SVs). The surgical field appears dry cranially, indicating good hemostasis. The surgeon discusses the use of quill suture for the next step, noting a needle break and replacing it with another quill suture.

So Jeff, post-operative things while we're having a bit of time here with the next quill. How long do you leave the catheter in for and do you do cystograms routinely? Very rarely do cystograms now. I did cystograms on my first two or three hundred patients. Now I only do them selectively. I do them all the time on salvage cases. But I typically won't do a cystogram I'm happy for the catheter to come out from day five onwards post-operatively. Normally it's a week because I'm back in the hospital privately. So if there's any issues with the patient, I'm there. So it's more a convenience measure than anything else. I have seen Alex Mottry describing early catheter removal on day two. And I have to admit I've just been a bit anxious about retention rates taking a catheter out that early. Do you have any little triggers not to take a catheter out? Perhaps blood in the catheter or... What would make you not take your catheter out routinely on day seven, five? I just can't remember when that last happened. I guess... The main issue I think would be if the patient had an acute early urine leak and had urine coming out the drain. But I just don't remember the last time I saw that happen. So typically I'll always get the catheter out on day seven. And even if there's a little bit of blood on and off, I still normally take the catheter out.

The surgeon performs posterior reconstruction by bringing the free edge of denim villias down to the tissue, just posterior to the urethra and the posterior rhabdo sphincter. The continuous suture technique is used, differing from the original description, and the second layer forms the posterior layer of the anastomosis.

Do you think using another side procedure such as quill at this particular point is a bit too traumatic on this posterior rapier sphincter? Sorry, V-lock, beg your pardon. V-lock does worry me. I didn't have a lot of trouble with the posterior sutures, but occasionally I'd look at it on the urethra and feel like it was cheese wiring the urethra a little bit, and I didn't like that. So before we had access to the quill, I'd moved back to monocryl from V-lock. But now with the quill, I feel it's a nice suture. You get some hold and purchase on the tissue, but I feel it's quite gentle on it.

If you do find you've got a big middle lobe and you're worried about the ureteric orifice, do you have a technique? Do you use infant feeding tubes? What's your technique under those circumstances at this particular point? Maybe three times ever I've put infant feeding tubes in. I normally just put a haemlock on the end so I don't lose them up the ureter. Generally, I find if you don't have to recept the UO and the Trigone for locally advanced disease, even if you're right on the inter-ureteric bar, you can sew your anastomosis in this fashion without the need for infant feeding tubes or stents, just paying attention to where they are without any trouble. So it's a rare day that... I'd do anything specific for the UOs. And you wouldn't revert to a posterior reconstruction because you're close to the UOs, you'd still directly suture the way you're doing here? Yeah, early on I'd do some lateral fish mouth closures like Vic does to push the UOs away, but now I find this technique, even if you're right on the interurotech bar, is never a problem.

I suppose if you can see them you're less likely to put a stitch through them? Yeah, exactly. So positioning, do you try and do a stitch every hour on the clock or is it just the way it feels, the way it comes together? Is there any sort of specific technique to how many sutures you'll put in your anastomosis? No, I don't think about it a lot, I have to admit. It probably does end up being somewhat like every hour on the clock. Patients where it's a bit tight and more difficult to get down adequately, I find they end up with more sutures and I've got a shorter suture at the end. But if they've come down nicely with that posterior layer that I've already done, I'll skip across the back wall pretty quickly and they might end up with ten or so passes through in total. Do you think it's important to take all of the bladder muscle in the bladder stitch or do you think it really matters? I don't know is the honest answer. I normally take full thickness bites and I take good bites on the urethra, good bites on the bladder. That's how Vic taught me and I've never had a reason not to do that. And the urethral bites, how far... down the urethra do you go with the bites? Does it think it really matters if you take smaller bites? I worry if you take too small a bite on the urethra, obviously it will pull through almost like the ultimate urethrotomy. wits that you tend to try and take with the urethral suture? I guess what I'm taking here, which I'd estimate is about five mils. I normally like to get a reasonable purchase on the urethra. I certainly don't want them to tear through. I certainly have seen Alex Mottry many years ago taking very tiny little bites and coming up through the dorsal vein. But this is how it taught me and I've been happy with it. I haven't had a reason to do anything different.

The surgeon discusses the technique of not suspending the dorsal venous complex (DVC) onto the pubis during the prostatectomy. Instead, they tie off the dorsal vein and take bites through the urethra and dorsal vein for stability during the anastomosis, without suspending it to the pubis.

Two clips are visible laterally on the bladder near the anastomosis site. The surgeon assesses their proximity to the anastomosis, considering whether to remove them if they are too close, while noting the low incidence of complications related to clip placement.

Particularly when I had a patient with one just in the middle of the trigone, away from the anastomosis, I'm not sure if some patients just react badly to it. But it's certainly rare to have a problem. Will you do a double A? Are you going to come back along the same? There you go. Okay. Answered my question. Pretty rough. I'll just whip up here without too much thought. So you're happy with the V-lock on the anterior bladder, clearly? Yeah, I'm quite happy that it's a little bit rougher there. Again, if I had the single arm quills, I think I'd prefer to use that. I'm not sure whether I'll get this up through the dorsal vein. My suture here that I've picked up isn't as long as what I routinely have at this point. I'll just jump across the other side. That's the point where I typically take a bite through the dorsal vein as well.

So normally you're saying you would put some sutures through the dorsal venous complex prior to sewing? I would, as I come across the front wall of the urethra from one side of that anterior racquet handle to the other, I'd put both of those sutures after I come through the urethra up through the sewn dorsal venous and then go back through the bladder and just elevate that anastomosis up towards the dorsal venous complex. But I've obviously taken a few more bites than normal. My suture was shorter, so I'll just do the anastomosis for him. And that's it. That is a fantastic case, Geoff. Well done. And to Vip, well trained. It's been a honour to have Geoff in Brisbane with us and you've been a wonderful teacher to him and I'm sure many urologists throughout the world. And Vip, again, on behalf of us here in Brisbane and I'm sure on behalf of many urologists throughout the world, thank you for your wonderful passion for urology and for teaching.
Yeah, thanks, Vip. Congratulations. Way to go. Well done, mate. Bye-bye. Cheers, mate. Bye.
